{{Short description|Kofun period burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]

==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[en:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:new pages]] [[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:shiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The area is now preserved as a park.

The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[en:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:new pages]] [[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:shiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ushiro Futago Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|burial mound]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[Sue ware]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[Meiji period]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[Meiji period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue ware pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Maebashi, Gunma]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} [[file:Shofutago-kofun 2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Sho-Futago Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast.
