{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi, Gunma]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|1=Sho Futago Kofun|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue pottery pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Omuro Park}} *[https://www.pref.gunma.jp/contents/100156310.pdf Gunma Prefecture official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1037 Meabashi City official site] {{in lang|ja}}
{{Kofun navbar}}

[[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Maebashi]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]]
[[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]
[[Category:Completion tier 5]]
}}

Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue pottery pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi, Gunma]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|1=Sho Futago Kofun|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue pottery pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[en:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[ja:後二子古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[en:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[ja:後二子古墳]] {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi, Gunma]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|1=Sho Futago Kofun|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue pottery pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Omuro Park}} *[https://www.pref.gunma.jp/contents/100156310.pdf Gunma Prefecture official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1037 Meabashi City official site] {{in lang|ja}}

The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]]. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue pottery pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]] in the northern [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|1=Sho Futago Kofun|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]s). After the discovery of the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]]. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high

It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue pottery pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue pottery pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[en:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[ja:後二子古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[en:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[ja:後二子古墳]] {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi, Gunma]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|1=Sho Futago Kofun|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|WD=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}} and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke}} and {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province}}s). After the discovery of the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}}. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue pottery pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Omuro Park}} *[https://www.pref.gunma.jp/contents/100156310.pdf Gunma Prefecture official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1037 Meabashi City official site] {{in lang|ja}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[en:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[ja:後二子古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[en:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[ja:後二子古墳]] {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi, Gunma]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}} in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|1=Sho Futago Kofun|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|WD=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}} and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke}} and {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province}}s). After the discovery of the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}}. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue pottery pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]]{{template-inline|Omuro Park}} *[https://www.pref.gunma.jp/contents/100156310.pdf Gunma Prefecture official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1037 Meabashi City official site] {{in lang|ja}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[en:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[ja:後二子古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[en:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[ja:後二子古墳]] {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi, Gunma]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|1=Sho Futago Kofun|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|WD=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|1=Tochigi Prefecture}} and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke|1=Kōzuke Province}} and {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province|1=Shimotsuke Province}}s). After the discovery of the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}}. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue pottery pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]]{{template-inline|Omuro Park}} *[https://www.pref.gunma.jp/contents/100156310.pdf Gunma Prefecture official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1037 Meabashi City official site] {{in lang|ja}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[en:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[ja:後二子古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[en:Ushirofutago Kofun]][[ja:後二子古墳]] {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | native_name =後二子古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Ushirofutago-kofun 1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ushiro-Futago Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|23|22|N|139|11|39|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebashi, Gunma]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = 1973 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (Park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Ushiro-Futago Kofun'''|後二子古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]] located in the Nishiomuromachi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maebashi, Gunma|Maebachi]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Together with the {{nihongo|'''Sho-Futago Kofun'''|小二子古墳|1=Sho Futago Kofun|}}, it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/137894|title=後二子古墳ならびに小古墳 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site dates from the late 6th century AD, and these tumuli are two of the five in the Ōmuro ''kofun'' cluster. The area is now preserved as a park.
==Ushirofutago Kofun== The Ushirofutago tumulus is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 110 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 106 meters. The lower tier is much larger than the upper tier, similar to the [[Azuma Kofun]] in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|WD=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|1=Tochigi Prefecture}} and this is a characteristic of tombs in the Kenō region (former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke|1=Kōzuke Province}} and {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province|1=Shimotsuke Province}}s). After the discovery of the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] in 1878, an application was issued to the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} to certify the tumulus as the tomb of King Mimorowake, a name which appears in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle as king of the Kenō region, but the application was denied due to lack of physical evidence.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
;Total length: 85 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 60 meters wide, 2-tier ;Posterior circular portion: 48 meter diameter x 11 meters high, 2-tiers
The burial chamber had a total length is nine meters, with a height of 2.2 meters high and width of 2.7 meters. A large amount of {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery, a long iron sword, short iron sword, horse harnesses, and some iron slag was found within the chamber as {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}}. Eleven earrings were also discovered, leading {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} researchers to conclude that at least six people were buried in this ''kofun''. Three human teeth were discovered, and were found to be that of a woman. In the vestibule, there was evidence that fires had been lit at at least three locations to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 400 ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' were found in various locations around the tumulus. Most were cylindrical, but several are decorated with bas-relief carvings of a monkey, a dog, or a dog chasing a monkey and its child. Such ''haniwa'' have been found at only three locations in Japan, all of which have been in Gunma.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Sho-Futago Kofun== This smaller tumulus has two tiers with a total length of 85 meters, and is orientated 131 degrees northeast. The tumulus partly utilizes a natural hill, which was cut away to contain a semi-underground stone-lined burial chamber. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' were not discovered. The tumulus had a horseshoe-shaped moat with a total length of 44 meters. The condition of the tumulus was poor, so it was fully excavated by archaeologists in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Total length: 38 meters ;Anterior rectangular portion: 18 meters wide ;Posterior circular portion: 30 meter diameter x 5 meters high
The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] has a total length is 69 meters, with a height of 1.8 meters high and width of 1.8 meters. It was formerly stone-lined, but the stones were stolen in the Meiji period. Grave goods include a small amount of Sue pottery pottery, 14 glass beads, 3 bronze earrings, 3 straight iron swords, 1 iron sword, 9 bow metal fittings, and some iron slag was found within. In the vestibule, as with the Ushirofutago Kofun, there was evidence that fires had been lit to cook food in a ritual banquet for the deceased. Some 80-90 cylindrical ''haniwa'' were found in various locations around the tumulus, including one in the shape of a person.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]]{{template-inline|Omuro Park}} *[https://www.pref.gunma.jp/contents/100156310.pdf Gunma Prefecture official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.maebashi-cvb.com/spot/1037 Meabashi City official site] {{in lang|ja}}
